meta.title: Portolan — Cloud-native spatial data infrastructure
meta.description: Portolan is a new foundation for sharing geospatial data: cloud-native files on object storage — no servers, no databases, no proprietary licenses. Structured metadata and open formats mean an agent can read a catalog, understand it, and analyze it directly. Open, AI-first, easy to implement, scalable, low cost, and sovereign.

hero.title: The future of spatial data
hero.description: Portolan is a new foundation for sharing geospatial data: cloud-native files on object storage — no servers, no databases, no proprietary licenses. Structured metadata and open formats mean an agent can read a catalog, understand it, and analyze it directly. Open, AI-first, easy to implement, scalable, low cost, and sovereign.

why.title: A cloud-native upgrade for spatial data infrastructure.
why.subtitle: SDIs are becoming more relevant than ever — but their requirements are changing.

open.title: Open
open.description: 100% open source under Apache-2.0, open formats, open governance. Geospatial data is data: DuckDB, BigQuery, and Pandas read the same bytes as desktop GIS. If Portolan disappeared tomorrow, your data would still work everywhere.

aiFirst.title: AI-first
aiFirst.description: Today's SDIs weren't built for AI. Portolan explains to agents in plain text files how to access the data — no API calls needed — and structured metadata tells them what it means. The <link>Finland.SDI demo</link> shows an agent answering questions against a national SDI.

easy.title: Easy to implement
easy.description: A traditional SDI needs databases, services, and specialist staff — a barrier most organizations can't clear. A Portolan node is files in a bucket: point, convert, push.

scalable.title: Infinitely scalable
scalable.description: Agents crawl entire datasets, not single queries. With no servers, scaling is handled entirely by cloud storage — the most robust, proven layer any cloud offers.

lowCost.title: Low cost
lowCost.description: Only two costs: storage and egress. Sharing public data shouldn't be an organizational burden, and popular datasets shouldn't cause budget shortfalls. The <link>cost calculator</link> shows what your data would run.

sovereign.title: Fully sovereign
sovereign.description: Control the full stack within your own jurisdiction — no foreign vendor in the stack. Host on AWS, GCS, Azure, MinIO, Hetzner, Scaleway, or any S3-compatible storage.

howItWorks.title: Point, convert, push.
howItWorks.subtitle: A community-built set of formats eliminates the need for a server — and scales through the power of cloud storage. No database, no tile server, no portal software: browsers, query engines, and AI agents read the files directly.

convert.title: Convert
convert.description: Transform shapefiles, GeoTIFFs, WFS, GeoPackage, and ArcGIS Feature Services into cloud-native formats: GeoParquet for vectors, COG for rasters, PMTiles for map tiles.

catalog.title: Catalog
catalog.description: Generate a Portolan catalog that describes every asset with structured metadata. It is built on STAC, so any STAC tool can read it.

publish.title: Publish
publish.description: Push the files to any S3-compatible object storage: AWS, GCS, Azure, R2, MinIO, or Source Cooperative for free open data hosting.

browse.title: Browse
browse.description: Open the result in the Portolan browser or any STAC viewer, query it with DuckDB, or point an agent at the catalog URL — data answers questions directly.

cli.name: portolan-cli
cli.title: The CLI
cli.description: Convert, validate, and sync. One command takes a folder of shapefiles to a browsable catalog on S3.
cli.compatibility: Works with AWS · GCS · Azure · R2 · MinIO · Source.coop

viewer.name: portolan-viewer
viewer.title: Browser-native viewer
viewer.description: A lightweight viewer that reads Portolan catalogs and cloud-native files directly, with no server behind it.

skills.name: portolan-skills
skills.title: AI agent skills
skills.description: Skills that let Claude, Gemini, and Codex agents publish catalogs and query cloud-native geospatial data.

quickstart.title: Quickstart
quickstart.intro: Browse an existing catalog, then publish your own data with the CLI or with an AI agent.

browse.title: Browse a catalog
browse.description: Portolan catalogs are live on the web. Open the browser to explore published datasets, or point DuckDB at any GeoParquet URL and query it in place.

cli.title: With the CLI
cli.description: Install portolan-cli, then init, add, check, and push. The CLI converts your files and generates the catalog.

claude.title: With Claude
claude.description: Install the Portolan skill in Claude Code and point it at your data. It handles conversion, metadata, and publishing — AI tools excel at CLIs and text files, so non-experts can publish too.

registry.title: Browse {count} catalogs
registry.description: Discover and connect to Portolan catalogs from around the world.

cta.title: Have a catalog to share?
cta.description: Submit your Portolan catalog to the registry.

submit.title: Submit a catalog
submit.successDescription: Your catalog will appear after review.

map.infoBody: Basemap © CARTO, © OpenStreetMap contributors. Search powered by Nominatim. Catalogs appear as dots; zoom in to see their coverage areas.
map.noLocation: {count, plural, one {# catalog has no location data} other {# catalogs have no location data}}

resources.title: Two talks that make the case.
resources.subtitle: Portolan is early-stage and developed in the open. Start here — the demos, the cost calculator, and the example catalogs are all linked from inside the talks.

holmesTalk.title: A Cloud-Native Geo Upgrade for Spatial Data Infrastructure
holmesTalk.description: Five requirements for a modern SDI, the cloud-native formats that meet them, and a live serverless portal for the Netherlands.

nextSdi.title: The Next SDI
nextSdi.attribution: Javier de la Torre · OGC Connect Helsinki
nextSdi.quote: “SDIs were built for experts; now they must serve agents.”
nextSdi.description: Why de-intermediation makes SDIs more important than ever, and the architecture they need — including the live Finland.SDI agent demo.
